title: Cve 2025 32463 LabX
description: Vulnerable Ubuntu 24.04 container with sudo 1.9.15p5. Non-privileged user (labuser). Exploit CVE-2025-32463 via the sudo-chwoot.sh script.
longDescription:
Vulnerable Ubuntu 24.04 container with sudo 1.9.15p5
Non-privileged user (labuser)
Exploit script: sudo-chwoot.sh
Docker
creator: Dock & Root Labs

title: Cve 2025 32463 Lab
description: Vulnerable Ubuntu 24.04 container with sudo 1.9.15p5. Community lab by 7r00t. Exploit the sudo chroot escape vulnerability.
longDescription:
Vulnerable Ubuntu 24.04 container with sudo 1.9.15p5
Non-privileged user (labuser)
Exploit script: sudo-chwoot.sh
Docker
creator: 7r00t

title: Injector
description: A vulnerable PHP login portal with user data stored in MySQL. Bypass authentication and extract the flag from the database.
longDescription:
A barebones PHP web application that talks directly to MySQL — no ORM, no parameterized queries, no input validation. The login form passes user-supplied input straight into a SELECT query, making it trivially injectable.

Your job: bypass authentication via SQL injection, enumerate the database schema, and extract the flag from a hidden secrets table.

### What you will practice
Classic in-band SQL injection, UNION-based data extraction, logical OR authentication bypass, and manual database enumeration without SQLMap.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Identify the vulnerable login parameter
Bypass authentication using a SQL logic bypass
Enumerate the database schema using UNION injection
Extract the flag from the secrets table

title: Escalate
description: Gain an initial foothold as a low-privilege user and escalate to root by chaining common Linux misconfigurations.
longDescription:
A realistic Debian-based machine where you start with SSH credentials for a low-privilege user. Multiple privilege escalation vectors are planted, including SUID binaries, weak sudo rules, cron job abuse, and world-writable scripts.

You are expected to enumerate the system, identify exploitable paths, and escalate to root without using Metasploit.

### What you will practice
Manual enumeration techniques (sudo -l, SUID find, cron inspection), PATH hijacking, and writing simple exploit scripts.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Connect to the target via SSH with provided credentials
Enumerate sudo rules, SUID binaries, and cron jobs
Identify at least one exploitable privilege escalation vector
Gain a root shell and read /root/flag.txt

title: Breakout
description: You are inside a privileged Docker container. Break out to the host filesystem and capture the root flag.
longDescription:
You start with a shell inside a Docker container running with elevated privileges. The challenge demonstrates how insecure container configurations — --privileged, exposed Docker socket, dangerous capabilities — can lead to full host compromise.

Chain together the misconfigurations to escape the container boundary, pivot to the host, and read the flag from the host's root directory.

### What you will practice
Container escape via privileged mode, Docker socket exploitation with nsenter, and understanding the Linux capability model.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Understand why --privileged is dangerous
Mount the host filesystem from inside the container
Escape the container namespace using nsenter
Read /root/flag.txt on the host machine

title: Cipher
description: Decrypt intercepted ciphertexts using classic and modern cryptographic attacks. Covers Caesar, XOR, and weak RSA.
longDescription:
Three encrypted messages are waiting. Each uses a progressively harder cipher: Caesar rotation, single-byte XOR, and textbook RSA with dangerously small primes.

No crypto library is required — brute force, frequency analysis, and basic math will get you through. The flag is assembled from fragments inside each decrypted message.

### What you will practice
Frequency analysis, XOR bruteforcing, RSA factor recovery with small primes, and Python scripting for crypto attacks.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Decrypt a Caesar-shifted ciphertext via frequency analysis
Recover the XOR key and decrypt the second message
Factor the RSA modulus and decrypt the third message
Assemble the three flag fragments

title: Smash
description: Smash the stack on a 32-bit Linux binary with no stack canary. Redirect execution to a win function and pop a shell.
longDescription:
A deliberately vulnerable C binary is running as a network service. It reads user input into a fixed-size stack buffer without bounds checking. No stack canary. NX disabled. Perfect for learning classic stack-based buffer overflows.

Find the offset to the return address, craft a payload, and redirect execution to the hidden win() function to get a shell and read the flag.

### What you will practice
GDB debugging, pattern offset calculation with pwntools, crafting a basic ROP chain, and writing a Python exploit script.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Identify the vulnerable buffer and compute the overflow offset
Locate the win() function address in the binary
Craft a payload that overwrites the return address
Get a shell on the remote service and read the flag

title: Wiretap
description: Analyze a PCAP capture of suspicious network traffic. Reconstruct the attack, extract credentials, and recover the hidden flag.
longDescription:
You are given a packet capture file from a compromised network segment. Inside: cleartext credential submission, a file transfer, and an exfiltrated secret hidden in DNS queries.

Use Wireshark and tshark to filter, reconstruct, and decode the traffic streams. No active exploitation required — this is pure packet analysis.

### What you will practice
Wireshark filters, TCP stream reconstruction, HTTP credential extraction, DNS-based data exfiltration detection, and file carving from PCAPs.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Identify the attacker's IP address and attack timeline
Extract the cleartext credentials from HTTP traffic
Reconstruct the transferred file from the PCAP
Decode the flag hidden inside DNS query names

title: Reversal
description: Analyze a stripped ELF binary that validates a password. Reverse the check logic and recover the correct password to get the flag.
longDescription:
A compiled Linux ELF binary prompts for a password. If correct, it prints the flag. If wrong, it exits silently. The binary is stripped — no debug symbols.

Use Ghidra or radare2 to decompile the main function, understand the password validation logic, and derive or brute-force the correct input.

### What you will practice
Static analysis in Ghidra, disassembly reading, understanding string operations in x86, and writing a keygen script.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Load the binary in Ghidra and navigate to main()
Understand the password comparison logic
Derive the correct password from the decompiled code
Run the binary with the correct password to reveal the flag

title: Infiltrator
description: Exploit a PHP LFI vulnerability to read sensitive system files and escalate to Remote Code Execution through log poisoning.
longDescription:
A PHP web application uses a ?page= parameter to include template files — without sanitizing path traversal sequences. The vulnerability is a textbook Local File Inclusion.

Start by reading /etc/passwd to confirm the vulnerability, then escalate to Remote Code Execution via Apache log poisoning to get a shell and read the flag.

### What you will practice
Path traversal, PHP wrappers (php://filter), log poisoning via User-Agent injection, and chaining LFI to RCE.
creator: Dock & Root Labs
objectives: Identify the LFI vulnerability in the page parameter
Read /etc/passwd via path traversal
Poison the Apache access log with PHP code via User-Agent
Trigger log inclusion to execute code and read the flag
